topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
A hierarchical, modular communication framework that enables scalable, interpretable emergent protocols through composable sub-language primitives, explicitly designed to generalize across varying agent population sizes and task complexities.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing emergent communication methods typically produce monolithic, opaque protocols that do not scale well to large multi-agent populations or complex, dynamic tasks, limiting practical applications in domains like swarm robotics and distributed sensor networks. The key open problem is how to construct communication schemes that remain efficient, interpretable, and generalizable as the number of agents and task intricacy grow, overcoming the scalability and interpretability bottlenecks that prior works have not resolved.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical communication architecture where agents learn to compose communication primitives into modular sub-languages organized by function or task context, enabling reuse and combinatorial generalization. The method integrates explicit structural constraints and inductive biases to encourage disentangled, interpretable communication components, combined with a multi-level reinforcement learning objective that optimizes both local sub-language efficacy and global task performance. This architecture employs an adaptive protocol compression mechanism to maintain bandwidth efficiency, dynamically adjusting communication granularity based on agent population size and task demands.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE), the StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC), and the Google Research Football Environment to rigorously test scalability and task complexity generalization. Baselines include the Biases for Emergent Communication (2019), Neuro-spike Communication framework (2025), and Learning to Cooperate (2020). Metrics are task success rate (targeting >5% absolute improvement over baselines), communication bandwidth reduction (aiming for ≥30% lower bits transmitted per timestep), and interpretability measured via modularity scores and human-annotated protocol coherence. Experiments involve training on small agent populations and testing zero-shot generalization to populations 2-3× larger, with 5 random seeds per setting and ablations on hierarchy depth and compression thresholds.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior approaches that generate single-layer, entangled communication protocols (Biases for Emergent Communication, Neuro-spike Communication), this work introduces explicitly composable, hierarchical communication languages that enable scalable reuse and clear semantic disentanglement, thus achieving zero-shot generalization to larger agent groups and more complex tasks unseen during training. This structured modularity for emergent communication is novel and has not been demonstrated to systematically improve both interpretability and scalability simultaneously.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a neuro-symbolic communication framework that dynamically adapts interpretable symbolic vocabularies for human-robot collaborative manufacturing, optimizing semantic transparency for humans while maintaining robustness and low-latency learning for neural agents under noisy, real-time sensory inputs.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods in multi-agent systems, including those aimed at human-robot collaboration, fail to produce symbolic vocabularies that are simultaneously semantically rich and human-interpretable, yet efficiently learnable by neural agents in noisy and latency-constrained manufacturing environments. This gap limits deployment in real-world settings where safety, efficiency, and operator trust critically depend on transparent and robust communication protocols that can operate effectively despite sensor noise and delays.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid neuro-symbolic communication protocol generator that combines neural message encoding with a symbolic vocabulary learning layer guided by human-centric semantic constraints and noise-resilience objectives. The system integrates a differentiable symbolic vocabulary module trained with multi-objective loss functions balancing interpretability (measured via semantic coherence with human labels), communication robustness (measured under simulated sensor noise and latency), and learning efficiency (via sample complexity). The communication protocol adapts dynamically via meta-learning to environmental noise profiles and operator feedback, enabling real-time adjustments to vocabulary granularity and symbolic expressiveness.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on the Meta-World robotic manipulation benchmark and the RoboCup@Work dataset for human-robot industrial collaboration, alongside the OpenAI Multi-Agent Particle Environment adapted for joint human-robot communication tasks. Baselines include the prior neuro-symbolic emergent communication system from “Human-Robot Collaborative Manufacturing,” the “Biases for Emergent Communication in Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning” (2019) model, and the “Firefly-inspired vocabulary generator” (2022). Metrics include semantic interpretability scores (>15% absolute improvement in human annotation agreement), communication robustness quantified by successful task completions under sensor noise (target >20% increase vs baseline), and latency reduction (aiming for >30% decrease in message decoding time). Experiments will run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs across 5 random seeds, with ablations on the symbolic vocabulary loss components and meta-learning adaptation frequency.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this method explicitly integrates human-interpretable symbolic constraints within a neuro-symbolic framework that adapts dynamically to sensor noise and latency in real time, enabling transparent yet robust communication protocols. This surpasses the static or purely neural emergent communication systems which either lack semantic transparency for humans or fail under realistic sensor noise conditions seen in manufacturing environments.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop adaptive communication protocols that dynamically optimize robustness and energy efficiency in heterogeneous, intermittently connected edge multi-agent systems by integrating context-aware noise estimation, resource-aware message compression, and decentralized coordination mechanisms.

BACKGROUND: Distributed edge computing networks suffer from unreliable, bandwidth-limited, and energy-constrained communication channels, especially under intermittent connectivity and device heterogeneity. Existing emergent communication research either assumes stable channels or does not explicitly optimize for real-time adaptation to variable noise, partial observability, and resource constraints, limiting coordination effectiveness and scalability in practical edge environments.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework where agents learn adaptive communication protocols composed of three key modules: (1) a context-aware noise estimator that infers current channel conditions from partial observations, (2) a resource-aware message encoder that compresses messages based on energy budget and bandwidth constraints, and (3) a decentralized coordination policy that dynamically selects communication partners and timing to maximize information flow under intermittent connectivity. The system jointly optimizes communication robustness and energy efficiency through a multi-objective reward function balancing task performance, message fidelity, and resource consumption.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on the AWS DeepRacer multi-agent benchmark for edge robotics coordination, the OpenAI Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) with added stochastic communication noise, and the NVIDIA AI City Challenge dataset simulating traffic edge devices under network variability. Metrics include >3% absolute improvement in task success rate over baselines, >25% reduction in total communication energy consumption, and >20% decrease in communication-induced latency. Experiments run on an 8-GPU cluster with 5 random seeds per configuration, including ablations isolating noise estimation, compression, and scheduling modules.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that assumes stable or simplified communication channels (e.g., fixed noise models or single-bit neuro-spike signals), our approach explicitly models and adapts to highly variable, intermittent edge network conditions in real time, optimizing both communication robustness and energy efficiency jointly—a capability not addressed by existing emergent communication or adaptive protocol methods.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a real-time, adaptive emergent communication framework for multi-agent autonomous vehicles that jointly optimizes information-theoretic message compression with goal-oriented protocol evolution to minimize latency and bandwidth while preserving safety-critical coordination under unreliable network conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods in multi-agent systems often do not explicitly address the stringent real-time constraints and unreliable network conditions faced by autonomous vehicles operating at the edge. Existing information-theoretic compression algorithms optimize bandwidth usage but fail to adapt dynamically to varying task goals and network states, risking loss of safety-critical information in cooperative vehicle navigation and decision-making. The open problem is to create a multi-agent communication protocol that simultaneously evolves interpretable, goal-directed messages while compressing sensory and decision data in real time, ensuring robust, low-latency coordination under network uncertainty.

APPROACH:  
Our method integrates an information-theoretic compression module based on adaptive variational bottlenecks with a reinforcement learning-driven emergent communication protocol that evolves discrete, goal-oriented message vocabularies in an end-to-end fashion. Compression rates and communication vocabularies co-adapt dynamically based on instantaneous network bandwidth, latency estimations, and task urgency signals within a multi-agent Markov game framework simulating autonomous vehicle coordination. We incorporate mutual information maximization between compressed messages and critical task variables to guarantee preservation of safety-relevant content while minimizing payload size. The communication protocol evolves using a neuro-inspired discrete-symbol generation mechanism with positive signaling and listening biases to enhance learning efficiency and message interpretability.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the CARLA autonomous driving simulator dataset combined with the Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) dataset and the KITTI Vision Benchmark Suite for multi-agent navigation and coordination tasks. Baselines include Real-Time Information-Theoretic Message Compression for Edge Computing (the tournament winner), CommNet, TarMAC, and IC3Net multi-agent communication architectures. Metrics: reduce end-to-end communication latency by ≥30%, decrease average bandwidth usage by ≥25%, and achieve ≥5% absolute improvement in task success rate (e.g., collision avoidance, route completion) compared to baselines. Training performed on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds; ablation studies on compression module, vocabulary evolution, and network-adaptive policy components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, which separately addresses either message compression or emergent communication protocols, our approach jointly optimizes adaptive information-theoretic compression with emergent, goal-oriented discrete communication under real-time constraints in unreliable edge networks. This enables dynamic trade-offs between communication efficiency and safety-critical information fidelity that previous methods—fixed compression or static protocols—cannot achieve simultaneously, advancing multi-agent emergent communication towards practical autonomous vehicle deployment.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
A scalable multimodal transformer-based foundation model architecture that jointly integrates genomic sequences, epigenomic profiles, and multimodal single-cell transcriptomic and spatial data across species to enable robust cross-modal representation learning and superior transfer to diverse downstream tasks in integrative genomics and single-cell biology.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell foundation models and genomics FMs predominantly handle isolated data modalities—such as transcriptomics or epigenomics—and often lack scalable architectures that unify highly heterogeneous data types including spatial transcriptomics and cross-species datasets. This limits their ability to capture complex biological interactions across modalities and species, reducing their effectiveness in comprehensive analyses such as cell type annotation, disease state prediction, and evolutionary comparisons. The open problem is to design a single foundation model that can seamlessly integrate these multiple, heterogeneous data sources at scale while maintaining interpretability and cross-species generalizability.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel hierarchical multimodal transformer architecture with modality-specific encoders for genomic sequences (e.g., DNA/RNA), epigenomic marks (e.g., ATAC-seq), and spatial transcriptomics, fused through a shared cross-modal attention mechanism. The model employs species-aware embedding layers and contrastive pretraining across modalities and species to learn unified biological representations. Training will leverage large-scale, diverse datasets to enable zero-shot and few-shot generalization across tissues, cell types, and organisms. Interpretability modules based on attention visualization and integrated gradients will link learned representations to biological features.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will train and evaluate on three benchmark datasets: (1) Human Cell Atlas (HCA) multi-omic single-cell data, (2) ENCODE epigenomics datasets including ATAC-seq and histone marks, and (3) Tabula Muris Senis spatial transcriptomics data. Baselines include CellBERT, Nephrobase Cell+, and scVI. Metrics will include >3% absolute improvement in cell type annotation F1-score, >5% increase in disease state prediction ROC-AUC, and cross-species transfer accuracy gains of at least 4%. Experiments will run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of ~30 days, conducting 5 random seed runs and ablation studies on cross-modal attention and species embeddings.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior single-cell or genomic foundation models that do not scale across multiple heterogeneous modalities and species simultaneously, this work introduces a unified architecture with explicit cross-modal and cross-species integration capabilities, yielding interpretable, scalable biological representations that demonstrably outperform state-of-the-art models on integrative, multimodal, and evolutionary tasks.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a self-supervised masked token modeling framework with adaptive regularization and rare cell-type-aware augmentation tailored for rare disease single-cell RNA-seq datasets to robustly learn biologically meaningful representations from limited, low-diversity samples.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell foundation models require large, diverse datasets and struggle to learn meaningful representations when rare cell types are underrepresented, as in rare disease cohorts. This limitation causes overfitting and poor generalization, hindering novel discovery of rare cell states and disease mechanisms in sparse single-cell data. There is a critical need for self-supervised learning methods specifically designed to handle data scarcity and imbalance in rare disease single-cell genomics.

APPROACH:  
We propose a self-supervised masked token modeling approach enhanced with adaptive regularization techniques such as dropout scheduling and contrastive loss that emphasize rare cell types. Additionally, we incorporate rare-cell-aware data augmentation via synthetic oversampling informed by lineage and gene regulatory networks. The model architecture integrates a transformer backbone pretrained on large single-cell atlases, fine-tuned using these tailored strategies to prevent overfitting and amplify subtle biological signals from rare cell subsets. Finally, we introduce a cell-type rarity weighting scheme in the loss function to bias representation learning toward underrepresented populations.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on three benchmark rare disease single-cell datasets: (1) the Human Cell Atlas Rare Disease single-cell subset, (2) the COVID-19 Single Cell Consortium data focusing on rare immune phenotypes, and (3) the Alzheimer’s Disease Single Nucleus RNA-seq (snRNA-seq) dataset. Baselines include scBERT, CellDART, and Nephrobase Cell+. Metrics will be adjusted Rand Index (ARI) for clustering rare cell types, F1-score for rare cell-type classification, and silhouette score for representation quality, targeting >3% absolute ARI improvement and >5% F1 increase over baselines. Experiments will run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, with five independent seeds, including ablations on augmentation strategies and rarity-weighting mechanisms.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior single-cell foundation models that assume large, balanced datasets, our method explicitly addresses low-diversity, scarce rare cell-type regimes by combining adaptive regularization, rare-cell-aware augmentation, and targeted loss weighting. This enables robust self-supervised representation learning where existing models fail, unlocking discovery potential in rare disease single-cell biology.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a differentiable neuro-symbolic foundation model for single-cell genomics that integrates curated biological pathway knowledge from heterogeneous symbolic databases to improve interpretability and predictive accuracy in cell-type classification and gene regulatory inference.

BACKGROUND: Current foundation models in single-cell biology inadequately leverage curated biological pathway knowledge because symbolic pathway databases (e.g., KEGG, Reactome, BioCyc) are incomplete and inconsistent, hindering their direct incorporation into neural architectures. This results in models that are less interpretable and sometimes less robust, as they rely purely on data-driven learning without grounding predictions in established biology. The open problem is how to create a flexible, end-to-end differentiable framework that incorporates symbolic pathway constraints while allowing the model to learn novel biological patterns from single-cell data.

APPROACH: We propose a neuro-symbolic hybrid architecture that encodes curated biological pathways as differentiable constraints within a graph neural network backbone pretrained on multimodal single-cell datasets. Symbolic pathway knowledge from KEGG, Reactome, and BioCyc will be represented as soft logic constraints embedded into the loss function and attention mechanisms, enabling the model to enforce known pathway interactions while learning data-driven adjustments. The model will jointly optimize predictive tasks such as cell-type annotation and gene regulatory network inference, maintaining end-to-end differentiability and permitting discovery of new pathway components and interactions. A pathway consistency regularizer will penalize deviations from known biology but allow flexibility for novel insights.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on three benchmark datasets: the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) single-cell RNA-seq dataset, the Tabula Muris Senis multimodal dataset, and the Kidney Precision Medicine Project (KPMP) single-cell dataset. Baselines include scBERT, Nephrobase Cell+, and the Pathway-GNN model. Metrics will include cell-type classification accuracy (target >5% absolute improvement over scBERT), gene regulatory network inference precision and recall (target >10% absolute F1 improvement), and pathway adherence scores quantifying consistency with curated pathway knowledge. Experiments will be run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 3 seeds for statistical robustness. Ablations will test the impact of each pathway database and the constraint regularization strength.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior single-cell foundation models and neuro-symbolic approaches that either ignore biological pathway inconsistencies or fail to maintain differentiability, our method flexibly integrates multiple curated pathway knowledge bases as soft, differentiable constraints within a unified neural architecture. This enables both improved interpretability grounded in biology and the capacity to learn novel biology, addressing the critical gap in combining symbolic biological knowledge with large-scale single-cell data modeling.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a novel end-to-end transformer compression framework integrating structured pruning, mixed-bit quantization, and multi-stage knowledge distillation tailored for single-cell genomics foundation models, achieving >5x memory compression with <2% diagnostic F1-score degradation on clinical single-cell tasks under constrained GPU memory (<4GB).

BACKGROUND:  
Single-cell foundation models (scFMs) enable powerful genomic and cellular biology insights but require large memory and compute resources, limiting on-site clinical deployment in resource-constrained hospital environments. Existing compression methods inadequately balance memory reduction and accuracy, often ignoring domain-specific characteristics of single-cell data and clinical diagnostic constraints. There is a critical need for a targeted compression pipeline that maintains predictive robustness and generalizability while enabling practical deployment on low-memory clinical devices.

APPROACH:  
We propose a transformer compression pipeline that (1) applies structured pruning at the attention-head and feed-forward sublayer granularity, guided by single-cell feature importance scores, (2) employs mixed-bit quantization optimized via quantization-aware training to preserve sensitive genomic feature representations, and (3) performs staged knowledge distillation from the full model to progressively compressed student models using multi-patient single-cell clinical datasets. The pipeline dynamically adapts compression ratios per layer and incorporates domain-specific loss regularization to maintain diagnostic F1-score fidelity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validate on multi-patient single-cell benchmark datasets including Human Cell Atlas (HCA), single-cell RNA-seq data from the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), and the Nephrobase Cell+ kidney single-cell dataset. Baselines include original uncompressed scBERT, LoRA-compressed transformers, and prior compression methods such as TinyBERT and DistilBERT adapted to genomics. Evaluate compression ratio (>5x target), diagnostic F1-score (target <2% absolute drop), and peak GPU memory utilization (<4GB) under identical training regimes (4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, 3 random seeds per experiment), including ablation studies isolating pruning, quantization, and distillation effects.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that treats transformer compression as a generic NLP or vision problem, this method explicitly integrates single-cell biological feature importance and clinical diagnostic constraints into compression decisions, enabling a higher compression-accuracy tradeoff. It surpasses existing scFM compression approaches by combining structured pruning, mixed-bit quantization, and multi-stage distillation tailored to genomics, achieving practical deployment feasibility on severely memory-limited clinical devices without sacrificing predictive performance.
